t: Early severe care disorder — neglect, multiple placement changes, institutionalism.
DSM-5-TR / ICD-11 — same criteria; DSED separate from RAD.
Developmental level by age must be considered — normal social interest in young children should not be confused with DSED.
Interview and observation — child's interaction with strangers.
Withdrawal, no attachment; open approach in DSED.
Impulsivity, but no social charm component.
Williams syndrome genetic test (FISH 7q11.23) — if suspected.
Parent training — developing a "safe base" concept for the child; ABC programs.
Community and school staff coordination — child safety.
There is no specific pharmacotherapy; it is for comorbid symptoms.
AACAP 2016 — same guidance as for RAD.
DSED, unlike RAD, can persist even in a stable environment — long-term follow-up.
Attachment and Biobehavioral Catch-up (ABC) — Dozier M.
Also used for RAD; in DSED strengthening caregiver-child synchronization.
Teaching child appropriate distance from strangers; reinforcement of caregiver-checking behavior.
In differentiation between RAD and DSED.
With stable care and intervention, improvement occurs; however, DSED sometimes persists long-term.
Impulsive behaviors and risks of abuse can persist into adulthood.
Myth 1: "An open, friendly child is a positive trait, not a disorder"
appropriate social distance is sign of developmental stage; in DSED absence of boundaries increases child's abuse risk.
Myth 2: "DSED resolves quickly in stable care"
In contrast to RAD, DSED sometimes persists even in a stable environment (Bucharest study); long-term intervention is required.
Myth 3: "DSED is a 'mild form' of autism"
distinct disorder; etiology psychosocial deprivation; clinical manifestations opposite (social withdrawal in autism, disinhibited approach in DSED).
Myth 4: "You can 'fix' a child with punishment"
Punishment exacerbates the violation and has a traumatic effect; a positive attachment-based approach is recommended.
Myth 5: "Williams syndrome and DSED are the same"
Williams syndrome genetic (7q11.23 deletion), specific physical and cognitive profile; "hypersociability" is present but it is a distinct neurobiological and etiological disorder.
WHO. ICD-11. 6B45 Disinhibited social engagement disorder. 2024.
Zeanah C.H. et al. AACAP Practice Parameter. JAACAP 2016;55(11):990–1003.
Gleason M.M. et al. Validity of evidence-derived criteria for reactive attachment disorder: indiscriminately social/disinhibited and emotionally withdrawn/inhibited types. JAACAP 2011;50(3):216–231.
Nelson C.A. et al. Bucharest Early Intervention Project. Science 2007;318(5858):1937–1940.
